student_name: Eliane Portela Nogueira Lima de Brito
hub_subtitle: Business English for a doctor rebuilding her professional voice

lesson.menu_title: The Present We Built — How Past Decisions Still Shape Today
lesson.menu_desc: Mixed conditionals (If I had taken the safe job, I would not be here now / If she weren't so honest, she would have hidden it) to talk about how past choices still shape the present you -- 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 18 -- The Present We Built
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Eliane Portela | Aula 18 -- The Present We Built: How Past Decisions Still Shape Today

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a
having a strong, lasting influence on how something develops
b
of central, crucial importance; the thing everything turns on
c
an event that shapes who or what someone becomes
d
the essential part on which everything else is built
e
a spreading series of effects from one single action
f
an indirect, secondary result of an action
g
to build up and grow greater over time
h
achieved only after a lot of effort and difficulty
i
the lasting result of past decisions or work
j
a deliberate change of direction to fix or improve a path
k
to return to where you started, but changed
l
a strategy focused on slow, distant, future rewards

paras: Eliane rarely talks about luck. When she looks at the clinic today, she sees the result of a handful of decisions that were far more formative than they seemed at the time. One of them was pivotal: years ago, against the advice of nearly everyone, she turned down a secure hospital post to specialize in reproductive medicine. If she had taken that safe job, she would not be running her own clinic now. That refusal became the defining moment of her career -- the cornerstone the whole practice was later built on.

Small choices compounded. A decision to invest early in one difficult procedure had a ripple effect she could not have predicted: it attracted the exact colleagues she needed, and their arrival had a knock-on effect on everything from research to reputation. Not every decision was right the first time. There was a hard-won course correction in the third year, when she quietly closed a service that was draining the team. If she weren't the kind of person who admits mistakes early, she would have carried that failure for much longer.

What Eliane understands now is that the present is always built out of the past. If she hadn't played the long game -- choosing slow, durable growth over quick wins -- the clinic would not have the reputation it enjoys today. In a way, her story has come full circle: the young doctor who once felt she had nothing to offer now trains the residents who will carry the work forward. That, she says, is the real legacy -- not the building, but the decisions that still shape everyone who walks through the door. If those early choices had been different, everything standing here would be different too.
reading.source: Adapted from a founder's essay on how the present is built, The Decisions Behind Us

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Eliane believes the clinic's success today is mostly the result of luck and good timing, not of any particular decisions.

The present is built out of past decisions: a few formative, pivotal choices -- and one hard-won course correction -- still shape the clinic today, which is why Eliane frames her story as decisions that have come full circle.
The text argues that Eliane regrets turning down the secure hospital job and wishes she had played it safe.

gapfill.text: Notice how the words trace how a present is built. A few formative, pivotal decisions become the cornerstone and the defining moment of a career. One choice compounds and has a ripple effect and a knock-on effect on everything else. A hard-won course correction fixes the path, and because you played the long game, the work finally comes full circle -- and that, not the building, is the legacy.

bank.label: Your reflection toolkit
items: If I had taken the safe job, I would not be running this clinic today.
If we hadn't played the long game, we would not have this reputation now.
If she weren't so honest about mistakes, she would have carried that failure for years.
If those early choices had been different, everything here would be different too.
That decision was the cornerstone; if we hadn't made it, we would not be here.
One choice compounded: it had a ripple effect on everything that came after.
The hard-won course correction saved the team; without it, we would still be struggling now.
If I didn't believe in slow growth, I would never have turned down the quick win.

lf.title: Mixed conditionals: name each half
items: A
If I
had taken
the safe job, I would not be here now.
strong
B
If I had taken the safe job, I
would not be
here now.
strong
C
If she
weren't
so honest, she would have hidden the mistake.
strong
D
If she weren't so honest, she
would have hidden
the mistake.
strong
E

had been

lf.text: These are mixed conditionals -- they connect two different times. A + B is past to present: an unreal PAST condition (if + past perfect: had taken) with a PRESENT result (would + base verb: would not be). C + D is present to past: an unreal PRESENT condition (if + past simple / were: weren't) with a PAST result (would have + past participle: would have hidden). The two halves point to different times, so the tenses do not match -- and there is never a 'would' in the if-clause.

items.situation: Think of a past decision that still shapes your work today. Use a past-to-present mixed conditional (If I had... , I would... now).
tips: If I had stayed at the hospital, I would not be running my own clinic now.
If we hadn't invested in that procedure early, we would have a much smaller practice today.

items.situation: Describe how a present trait or belief changed something in the past. Use a present-to-past mixed conditional (If I weren't... , I would have...).
tips: If I weren't so stubborn, I would have given up in the first year.
If she didn't care so much about her patients, she would never have built this.

items.situation: Name a pivotal decision and its ripple effect. Use 'cornerstone' or 'defining moment'.
tips: Turning down that job was the defining moment; it became the cornerstone of everything.
That one pivotal choice had a ripple effect on the whole team.

items.situation: Explain a hard-won course correction and where you would be without it. Use a mixed conditional.
tips: If we hadn't made that course correction, we would still be losing money today.
The correction was hard-won; without it, the clinic would not be profitable now.

items: Round 1 -- The decision that still shapes you
Tell one formative, pivotal decision from your past and how it shapes your present. Use a past-to-present mixed conditional: If I had (not) ... , I would (not) ... now.
Round 2 -- Who you are, and what it changed
Describe a trait or value you have now and how it changed a past outcome. Use a present-to-past mixed conditional: If I weren't / didn't ... , I would (never) have ... .
Round 3 -- Ripple effects
Choose one choice and trace its ripple effect and knock-on effect on everything that followed. Name the cornerstone or defining moment.
Round 4 -- Full circle
Put it together in one minute: the formative choice, the hard-won course correction, the long game, and how the work has come full circle into your legacy.

answerkey.title: Reveal the model language
list: The decision that still shapes you: 'If I had taken the secure hospital job, I would not be running my own clinic today -- that refusal was the defining moment of my career.'
Who you are, and what it changed: 'If I weren't the kind of person who admits mistakes early, I would have carried that failure for years instead of correcting it.'
Ripple effects: 'Investing in one difficult procedure was the cornerstone; it had a ripple effect that attracted the colleagues I needed, and a knock-on effect on our whole reputation.'
Full circle: 'I played the long game, made one hard-won course correction, and now the work has come full circle -- the real legacy is the decisions that still shape everyone who walks in.'
answerkey.note: Notice the two mixed patterns: past to present (If I had taken ... I would not be) and present to past (If I weren't ... I would have carried). The tenses in the two halves point to different times, and there is never a 'would' in the if-clause.

listenings.text: Eliane, it's Adrian. I was going through the old board minutes tonight and it stopped me. Do you remember the meeting where you told us you were turning down the hospital post? Half the room thought you were making a mistake. Looking back, that was the pivotal one. If you had listened to us back then, you would be a comfortable department head somewhere, and none of this would exist. That refusal was the cornerstone of everything that followed. And the course correction in year three, closing that service, that was hard-won, but if you hadn't had the honesty to do it, we would still be losing money today. I just wanted you to hear someone say it out loud: the present you built came from those decisions. Anyway, call me back. I would like to talk about the long game for the next five years.

When residents ask me how the clinic came to be, I don't start with the building. I start with a decision. Years ago, I turned down a safe hospital post, and if I had taken it, I would not be standing here now. That one choice was formative; it became the defining moment everything else was built on. It compounded in ways I never planned: it had a ripple effect that brought the right people to me, and a knock-on effect on our research and our reputation. There was one hard-won course correction along the way, and honestly, if I weren't willing to admit mistakes early, I would have wasted years. I played the long game, and slowly the work has come full circle. The real legacy isn't the clinic; it's the decisions that still shape everyone who walks through that door.
